summary: High‑quality FM broadcast of the legendary 6/12/76 Boston show – superb performance (Samson & Delilah, Mission, Big River) captured with clear sound despite modest hiss and occasional distortion.
review: The recording is an FM‑broadcast tape that sounds surprisingly clean for a radio source; reviewers note a pleasant mix with a little background noise but overall good fidelity. Musically the concert shines – Jerry’s mid‑’76 Travis Bean tone, Keith’s soaring keys and strong contributions from Phil, Bob and Bill are highlighted, especially on Samson & Delilah, Mission in the Rain, Big River, Cassidy and Comes a Time. The set flows with an electrifying first set opener and a second set that, while mixed in consistency, delivers several memorable peaks.

ai_rating.rationale: Most reviewers give five stars and praise both the performance and the recording; only one review is mildly critical of a few songs or minor noise, yielding a strong overall rating.

recording_quality.technical_notes: FM‑broadcast source with clear mix and decent instrument separation; occasional hiss/noise and brief distortion noted but not disruptive.

show_quality.setlist_flow: First set launches with a powerful Samson opener and strong early numbers, then settles into an average middle; second set is uneven but contains high points (Cassidy, Comes a Time) and a lively close.

band_member_comments.Jerry: His mid‑’76 Travis Bean tone is repeatedly praised; Garcia’s noodling, storytelling and vocal backing receive strong compliments.
band_member_comments.Phil: Bass work highlighted as solid and driving; Phil’s opening notes on "Lazy Lightning" noted positively.
band_member_comments.Bob: Weir’s vocals and lyric delivery are mentioned (e.g., on Looks Like Rain); occasional comment about sharing spotlight but overall positive.
band_member_comments.Keys: Keith’s playing is called “amazing” on Friend of the Devil and praised throughout; keyboards sit well in the mix.
band_member_comments.Drums: The drummers are credited for energizing the crowd, with remarks that they could “bring the entire Garden to the ground.”
